---
title: "NFL Draft Quasi-Sharpe Ratio Analysis"
subtitle: "How much is a draft pick really worth, by position?"
output:
  github_document:
    html_preview: false
knit: (function(input, ...) rmarkdown::render(input, output_dir = dirname(input), ...))
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo = FALSE, warning = FALSE, message = FALSE,
  fig.width = 10, fig.height = 7, dpi = 300,
  fig.path = "charts/"
)
library(tidyverse)
library(arrow)
library(scales)
library(ggridges)
library(gt)
library(nflplotR)
library(nflreadr)

pos_colors <- c(
  "QB" = "#E41A1C", "RB" = "#377EB8", "WR" = "#4DAF4A", "TE" = "#984EA3",
  "OT" = "#FF7F00", "IOL" = "#FFFF33", "EDGE" = "#A65628", "DL" = "#F781BF",
  "LB" = "#999999", "CB" = "#66C2A5", "S" = "#FC8D62"
)

tier_order <- c("Top 10", "Late 1st", "Day 2", "Day 3 Early", "Day 3 Late")
```

```{r load-data}
df <- read_parquet("data/draft_sharpe_analysis.parquet")
sharpe <- read_csv("output/sharpe_ratios.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
fa_replacement <- read_csv("output/fa_replacement.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)
player_lookup <- read_csv("output/player_lookup.csv", show_col_types = FALSE)

sharpe <- sharpe %>% mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = tier_order))
df <- df %>% mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = tier_order))
player_lookup <- player_lookup %>% mutate(tier = factor(tier, levels = tier_order))

# Load headshots and most recent team for gt tables
rosters <- load_rosters(seasons = 2012:2025)

headshots <- rosters %>%
  select(gsis_id, headshot_url) %>%
  distinct(gsis_id, .keep_all = TRUE)

recent_teams <- rosters %>%
  filter(!is.na(gsis_id), gsis_id != "") %>%
  group_by(gsis_id) %>%
  slice_max(season, n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(gsis_id, recent_team = team)

player_lookup <- player_lookup %>%
  left_join(headshots, by = "gsis_id") %>%
  left_join(recent_teams, by = "gsis_id") %>%
  mutate(team = coalesce(recent_team, team)) %>%
  select(-recent_team)
```

## The Setup

Traditional draft pick valuation treats picks as interchangeable assets — a "top 10 pick" has
one value regardless of whether you're drafting a quarterback or a running back. But the return
on investment varies enormously by position. A quasi-Sharpe ratio lets us quantify this.

This analysis combines two frameworks:

1. **Hit Rate ([Riske/PFF](https://www.pff.com/news/draft-pff-data-study-what-makes-an-nfl-draft-pick-a-hit)):**
   A player is a "hit" if their average snap percentage over their first 4 NFL seasons reaches at
   least 2/3 of the positional baseline for a full-time starter. Snap % is averaged over ALL
   expected games (including seasons not played as zero), so players who wash out are properly
   penalized.

2. **Nonlinear Upside ([Brill & Wyner 2024](https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.00730)):** Not all hits are
   equal. Variance decays convexly across the draft, meaning earlier picks have fatter right tails.
   We capture this with a **snap-weighted return** — snap ratio (playing time relative to positional
   baseline) times second-contract value (APY as % of salary cap). This rewards players who both
   dominated snaps AND earned elite second contracts, while filtering out players who got paid but
   didn't play.

3. **Salary data** from [OverTheCap](https://overthecap.com/contracts) via
   [nflreadr](https://nflreadr.nflverse.com/). Contract values normalized to % of salary cap to
   control for cap inflation across eras.

**The formula:**

**Sharpe = (P(elite snap-weighted return) x Elite Threshold - FA Replacement Cost) / Return Volatility**

| Component | What it measures |
|---|---|
| **Return** | Probability of a top-10% snap-weighted return (snap ratio x contract cap%) at that position x tier |
| **Risk-free rate** | Median starter-caliber FA contract for that position ([top 32 per year](https://overthecap.com/contracts)) |
| **Volatility** | Std dev of snap-weighted returns, including busts at $0 |

A high ratio = reliable surplus above free agency. A low or negative ratio = you're better off
buying the position in free agency and using the draft capital elsewhere.

**Data sources:** Draft picks, snap counts, and contracts all via
[nflreadr](https://nflreadr.nflverse.com/) (PFR + OverTheCap). Analysis covers all drafted
players from 2009 onward where snap count data is available.

```{r elite-threshold}
# Elite threshold uses snap-weighted return (snap_ratio × contract)
df <- df %>% mutate(
  snap_ratio = avg_snap_pct / hit_threshold,
  player_return = snap_ratio * second_apy_cap_pct
)
elite_threshold <- quantile(df$player_return[df$player_return > 0], 0.90)
```

## Hit Rates by Position and Tier

How often does a draft pick become a starter-caliber player? This heatmap shows hit rates
(players exceeding the snap % threshold) by position group and draft tier.

```{r hit-rate-heatmap, fig.height=8}
hit_rates <- sharpe %>%
  select(pos_group, tier, hit_rate, n)

ggplot(hit_rates, aes(x = tier, y = reorder(pos_group, hit_rate), fill = hit_rate)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(hit_rate * 100), "%\n(n=", n, ")")),
            size = 3.2, color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#c62828", mid = "#f9a825", high = "#2e7d32",
    midpoint = 0.5, limits = c(0, 1),
    labels = percent_format()
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Draft Hit Rates by Position Group and Tier",
    subtitle = "Hit = average snap % exceeds position-specific threshold",
    x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Hit Rate"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r hit-rate-table}
hit_rates %>%
  mutate(hit_rate = percent(hit_rate, accuracy = 0.1)) %>%
  select(-n) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = tier, values_from = hit_rate) %>%
  rename(Position = pos_group) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

## The Nonlinearity Story

Second-contract values are not normally distributed. The ridgeline plot below shows how
snap-weighted returns (snap ratio x contract value) cluster near zero for most players, with
long right tails for positions like QB and EDGE. By weighting by snap ratio, players who
earned big contracts but barely played are discounted.

```{r ridgeline-plot, fig.height=8}
df_contracts <- df %>%
  filter(player_return > 0)

ggplot(df_contracts, aes(x = player_return, y = reorder(pos_group, player_return, FUN = median), fill = pos_group)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7, scale = 1.5, rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = elite_threshold, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.7) +
  annotate("text", x = elite_threshold + 0.005, y = 1, label = paste0("Elite threshold: ", percent(elite_threshold, accuracy = 0.1)),
           hjust = 0, size = 3.5, color = "red") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pos_colors) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Distribution of Snap-Weighted Returns by Position",
    subtitle = "Snap Ratio x Second Contract Cap% | Dashed line = 90th percentile (elite)",
    x = "Snap-Weighted Return (Snap Ratio x APY Cap%)", y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

```{r elite-probability, fig.height=6}
key_positions <- c("QB", "RB", "WR", "TE", "EDGE", "OT")

# Compute Wilson binomial confidence intervals for elite probability
# Elite = top 10% of snap-weighted return (snap_ratio × contract)
elite_by_tier <- df %>%
  group_by(pos_group, tier) %>%
  summarise(
    n = n(),
    successes = sum(player_return >= elite_threshold),
    elite_prob = successes / n,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    # Wilson score interval
    z = 1.96,
    denom = 1 + z^2 / n,
    center = (elite_prob + z^2 / (2 * n)) / denom,
    margin = z * sqrt((elite_prob * (1 - elite_prob) + z^2 / (4 * n)) / n) / denom,
    ci_lo = pmax(center - margin, 0),
    ci_hi = pmin(center + margin, 1)
  ) %>%
  filter(pos_group %in% key_positions)

ggplot(elite_by_tier, aes(x = tier, y = elite_prob, color = pos_group, fill = pos_group, group = pos_group)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), alpha = 0.12, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(values = pos_colors) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pos_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format()) +
  labs(
    title = "Probability of an Elite Snap-Weighted Return by Position and Tier",
    subtitle = "Elite = top 10% of Snap Ratio x Contract Cap% | Bands = 95% Wilson CI",
    x = NULL, y = "P(Elite)", color = "Position"
  ) +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

## Free Agency Replacement Cost

The "risk-free rate" in our Sharpe ratio. Instead of drafting a player, a team could sign a
starter-caliber free agent. To measure this, we take the **top 32 contracts per position per
year** (one starter per team) and compute the median APY as a percentage of the salary cap.
This filters out vet-minimum and practice squad deals that would artificially deflate the
replacement cost. The result is what a team actually pays to acquire a real starter on the
open market — the true opportunity cost of using a draft pick instead.

```{r fa-replacement-bar, fig.height=6}
ggplot(fa_replacement, aes(x = reorder(pos_group, fa_median_apy_cap_pct), y = fa_median_apy_cap_pct, fill = pos_group)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  geom_text(aes(label = percent(fa_median_apy_cap_pct, accuracy = 0.1)),
            hjust = -0.2, size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pos_colors) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = percent_format(), expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.15))) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title = "Free Agency Replacement Cost by Position",
    subtitle = "Median APY as % of salary cap for FA signings",
    x = NULL, y = "Median APY (% of Cap)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(legend.position = "none")
```

## The Sharpe Ratio

The money shot. This heatmap shows the quasi-Sharpe ratio for every position-tier combination.
Higher values mean better risk-adjusted returns relative to free agency replacement cost.

```{r sharpe-heatmap, fig.height=8}
ggplot(sharpe, aes(x = tier, y = reorder(pos_group, sharpe_elite), fill = sharpe_elite)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(sharpe_elite, 2)),
            size = 3.5, color = "white", fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_gradient2(
    low = "#c62828", mid = "#f9a825", high = "#2e7d32",
    midpoint = 0
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Quasi-Sharpe Ratio by Position Group and Draft Tier",
    subtitle = "Snap-weighted return vs FA replacement cost | Higher = better risk-adjusted return",
    x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Sharpe\nRatio"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1),
    panel.grid = element_blank()
  )
```

```{r sharpe-table}
sharpe %>%
  select(pos_group, tier, n, hit_rate, elite_prob, mean_player_return, fa_replacement, sharpe_elite) %>%
  mutate(
    hit_rate = percent(hit_rate, accuracy = 0.1),
    elite_prob = percent(elite_prob, accuracy = 0.1),
    mean_player_return = round(mean_player_return, 4),
    fa_replacement = percent(fa_replacement, accuracy = 0.01),
    sharpe_elite = round(sharpe_elite, 3)
  ) %>%
  rename(
    Position = pos_group, Tier = tier, N = n, `Hit Rate` = hit_rate,
    `P(Elite)` = elite_prob, `Mean Snap-Wtd Return` = mean_player_return,
    `FA Replacement` = fa_replacement,
    `Sharpe Ratio` = sharpe_elite
  ) %>%
  knitr::kable()
```

## Sharpe Ratio Curves

How does the Sharpe ratio decay as you move to later draft tiers? Some positions hold value
deep into Day 2 and 3, while others collapse quickly.

```{r sharpe-curves, fig.height=7}
# Bootstrap 95% CI for Sharpe ratio by position x tier
set.seed(42)
n_boot <- 1000

sharpe_boot <- df %>%
  filter(pos_group %in% key_positions) %>%
  group_by(pos_group, tier) %>%
  summarise(
    data = list(player_return),
    fa = first(fa_median_apy_cap_pct),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    boot = map2(data, fa, function(d, fa_val) {
      replicate(n_boot, {
        s <- sample(d, length(d), replace = TRUE)
        ep <- mean(s >= elite_threshold, na.rm = TRUE)
        (ep * elite_threshold - fa_val) / sd(s)
      })
    }),
    sharpe = map_dbl(boot, ~ mean(.x[is.finite(.x)], na.rm = TRUE)),
    ci_lo = map_dbl(boot, ~ quantile(.x[is.finite(.x)], 0.025, na.rm = TRUE)),
    ci_hi = map_dbl(boot, ~ quantile(.x[is.finite(.x)], 0.975, na.rm = TRUE))
  )

sharpe_boot <- sharpe_boot %>%
  mutate(
    ci_lo = pmax(ci_lo, -3),
    ci_hi = pmin(ci_hi, 3)
  )

ggplot(sharpe_boot, aes(x = tier, y = sharpe, color = pos_group, fill = pos_group, group = pos_group)) +
  geom_ribbon(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), alpha = 0.12, color = NA) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "gray40", linewidth = 0.6) +
  scale_color_manual(values = pos_colors) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pos_colors) +
  coord_cartesian(ylim = c(-2.5, 1.5)) +
  labs(
    title = "Sharpe Ratio by Draft Tier",
    subtitle = "Snap-weighted | Bands = 95% bootstrap CI | Dashed line = break-even vs. free agency",
    x = NULL, y = "Quasi-Sharpe Ratio", color = "Position"
  ) +
  guides(fill = "none") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 14) +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))
```

## So Should You Draft Jeremiah Love at 10?

```{r love-hook, results='asis'}
rb_sharpe <- sharpe %>% filter(pos_group == "RB")
rb_top10 <- rb_sharpe %>% filter(tier == "Top 10")
rb_day2 <- rb_sharpe %>% filter(tier == "Day 2")
rb_fa <- fa_replacement %>% filter(pos_group == "RB")

# Position with steepest drop-off
dropoffs <- sharpe %>%
  filter(tier %in% c("Top 10", "Day 2")) %>%
  select(pos_group, tier, sharpe_elite) %>%
  pivot_wider(names_from = tier, values_from = sharpe_elite) %>%
  mutate(dropoff = `Top 10` - `Day 2`) %>%
  filter(!is.na(dropoff)) %>%
  arrange(desc(dropoff))

steep_pos <- dropoffs %>% slice(1)

cat(paste0(
  "Now we can answer the question. Top-10 RBs hit at a high rate ",
  "(**", percent(rb_top10$hit_rate, accuracy = 0.1), "**), but the Sharpe ratio is ",
  "**", round(rb_top10$sharpe_elite, 2), "** — middle of the pack for top-10 picks.\n\n",

  "The real issue is that you can get nearly the same return two rounds later. ",
  "A Day 2 RB produces a Sharpe ratio of **", round(rb_day2$sharpe_elite, 2), "** — ",
  "the dropoff from top 10 to Day 2 is just **",
  round(rb_top10$sharpe_elite - rb_day2$sharpe_elite, 2), "**. ",
  "Compare that to ", steep_pos$pos_group, ", where the top-10-to-Day-2 dropoff is **",
  round(steep_pos$dropoff, 2), "**. The marginal value of a top-10 pick on a ",
  steep_pos$pos_group, " is enormous; on a running back, it's modest.\n\n",

  "Meanwhile, RBs are the cheapest position to replace in free agency — a starter-caliber RB ",
  "costs just **", percent(rb_fa$fa_median_apy_cap_pct, accuracy = 0.1), " of the salary cap**, ",
  "the lowest of any position. So not only is the draft capital largely wasted relative to other ",
  "positions, but the alternative (signing an RB in free agency) is dirt cheap.\n"
))
```

## Player Lookup

Every drafted player scored with a **player-level Sharpe ratio** that rewards both sustained
playing time (snap ratio vs positional baseline) and second-contract quality. Higher values
mean a player delivered more risk-adjusted return relative to what a free-agent starter would
have cost.

**Player Sharpe = (Snap Ratio x Second Contract Cap% - FA Replacement Cost) / Bucket Volatility**

```{r player-lookup-table, results='asis'}
make_player_gt <- function(data, title, subtitle = NULL) {
  tbl <- data %>%
    mutate(rank = row_number()) %>%
    select(rank, gsis_id, pfr_player_name, pos_group, team,
           season, pick, snap_ratio, second_apy_cap_pct, player_sharpe)

  tbl %>%
    gt() %>%
    gt_nfl_headshots(columns = "gsis_id", height = 35) %>%
    gt_nfl_wordmarks(columns = "team", height = 25) %>%
    cols_label(
      rank = "",
      gsis_id = "",
      pfr_player_name = "PLAYER",
      pos_group = "POS",
      team = "TEAM",
      season = "YEAR",
      pick = "PICK",
      snap_ratio = "SNAP RATIO",
      second_apy_cap_pct = "2ND CONTRACT",
      player_sharpe = "PLAYER SHARPE"
    ) %>%
    fmt_number(columns = c(snap_ratio, player_sharpe), decimals = 2) %>%
    fmt_percent(columns = second_apy_cap_pct, decimals = 2) %>%
    data_color(
      columns = player_sharpe,
      palette = c("#f7f7f7", "#c6dbef", "#4292c6", "#08519c"),
      domain = NULL
    ) %>%
    data_color(
      columns = snap_ratio,
      palette = c("#f7f7f7", "#c7e9c0", "#41ab5d", "#006d2c"),
      domain = NULL
    ) %>%
    data_color(
      columns = second_apy_cap_pct,
      palette = c("#f7f7f7", "#dadaeb", "#9e9ac8", "#6a51a3"),
      domain = NULL
    ) %>%
    tab_header(title = title, subtitle = subtitle) %>%
    tab_style(
      style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
      locations = cells_column_labels()
    ) %>%
    tab_style(
      style = cell_text(size = px(13)),
      locations = cells_body()
    ) %>%
    tab_options(
      table.font.size = px(13),
      heading.title.font.size = px(18),
      heading.subtitle.font.size = px(13),
      column_labels.font.size = px(11),
      data_row.padding = px(4)
    )
}

top25 <- player_lookup %>%
  filter(!is.na(player_sharpe)) %>%
  arrange(desc(player_sharpe)) %>%
  head(25)

top25_gt <- make_player_gt(
  top25,
  title = "Top 25 Draft Picks by Player-Level Sharpe Ratio",
  subtitle = "Snap Ratio = avg snap % / positional baseline | 2nd Contract = APY as % of salary cap"
)

gtsave(top25_gt, filename = "charts/player_lookup_top25.png", vwidth = 900)
cat("![Top 25 Draft Picks by Player-Level Sharpe Ratio](charts/player_lookup_top25.png)\n")
```

## Key Takeaways

```{r takeaways, results='asis'}
# Best top-10 value
best_top10 <- sharpe %>%
  filter(tier == "Top 10") %>%
  arrange(desc(sharpe_elite)) %>%
  slice(1)

# Worst top-10 value
worst_top10 <- sharpe %>%
  filter(tier == "Top 10") %>%
  arrange(sharpe_elite) %>%
  slice(1)

# Best Day 2 value
best_day2 <- sharpe %>%
  filter(tier == "Day 2") %>%
  arrange(desc(sharpe_elite)) %>%
  slice(1)

# Position that stays positive longest
stays_positive <- sharpe %>%
  filter(sharpe_elite > 0) %>%
  group_by(pos_group) %>%
  summarize(deepest_positive = max(as.numeric(tier)), .groups = "drop") %>%
  arrange(desc(deepest_positive)) %>%
  slice(1)

cat(paste0(
  "- **Best top-10 Sharpe ratio**: ", best_top10$pos_group,
  " (", round(best_top10$sharpe_elite, 2), ") -- ",
  "drafting a ", best_top10$pos_group, " in the top 10 offers the best risk-adjusted return.\n\n",

  "- **Worst top-10 Sharpe ratio**: ", worst_top10$pos_group,
  " (", round(worst_top10$sharpe_elite, 2), ") -- ",
  "top-10 ", worst_top10$pos_group, " picks have the weakest return relative to variance.\n\n",

  "- **Best Day 2 value**: ", best_day2$pos_group,
  " (", round(best_day2$sharpe_elite, 2), ") -- ",
  "second-round ", best_day2$pos_group, " picks offer the strongest mid-round return.\n\n",

  "- **Deepest value**: ", stays_positive$pos_group,
  " maintains a positive Sharpe ratio deeper into the draft than any other position, ",
  "suggesting sustained value even with later picks.\n"
))
```

---

## Appendix: Round 1 Leaderboard

The overall leaderboard is dominated by late-round steals. Here are the best first-round picks
by player Sharpe — the guys who actually justified their draft capital.

```{r round1-leaderboard, results='asis'}
rd1 <- player_lookup %>%
  filter(round(pick) <= 32, !is.na(player_sharpe)) %>%
  arrange(desc(player_sharpe)) %>%
  head(25)

rd1_gt <- make_player_gt(
  rd1,
  title = "Top 25 Round 1 Picks by Player Sharpe",
  subtitle = "First-round picks only | Snap Ratio x Contract relative to FA replacement cost"
)

gtsave(rd1_gt, filename = "charts/player_lookup_rd1.png", vwidth = 900)
cat("![Top 25 Round 1 Picks by Player Sharpe](charts/player_lookup_rd1.png)\n")
```

## Appendix: Top 5 by Position Group

```{r position-leaderboards, results='asis'}
pos_groups <- sort(unique(player_lookup$pos_group))

for (pg in pos_groups) {
  top5 <- player_lookup %>%
    filter(pos_group == pg, !is.na(player_sharpe)) %>%
    arrange(desc(player_sharpe)) %>%
    head(5)

  if (nrow(top5) == 0) next

  tbl <- make_player_gt(
    top5,
    title = paste("Top 5", pg, "by Player Sharpe"),
    subtitle = "All rounds"
  )

  fname <- paste0("top5_", tolower(pg), ".png")
  gtsave(tbl, filename = paste0("charts/", fname), vwidth = 900)
  cat(paste0("![Top 5 ", pg, "](charts/", fname, ")\n\n"))
}
```

## Appendix: Top 5 by Position Group (Round 1 Only)

```{r position-leaderboards-rd1, results='asis'}
for (pg in pos_groups) {
  top5_rd1 <- player_lookup %>%
    filter(pos_group == pg, pick <= 32, !is.na(player_sharpe)) %>%
    arrange(desc(player_sharpe)) %>%
    head(5)

  if (nrow(top5_rd1) == 0) next

  tbl <- make_player_gt(
    top5_rd1,
    title = paste("Top 5", pg, "by Player Sharpe (Round 1)"),
    subtitle = "First-round picks only"
  )

  fname <- paste0("top5_rd1_", tolower(pg), ".png")
  gtsave(tbl, filename = paste0("charts/", fname), vwidth = 900)
  cat(paste0("![Top 5 ", pg, " Round 1](charts/", fname, ")\n\n"))
}
```
